reasoning: The injustice is clear because the moderator's actions explicitly contradict the written rules of the community they are appointed to serve. A reasonable person would agree that a delegate authority loses legitimacy when they enforce personal vendettas that violate the specific policies they are authorized to uphold.

Retroactively disabling core functionality on hardware purchased outright to force a new subscription is widely viewed as a violation of property rights and consumer trust. A reasonable person would agree that the vendor lacks the legitimate authority to 'hold ransom' physical property the user already owns.

Demanding access to a worker's private photo gallery and entire contact list as a condition for a package delivery job is a clear overreach of the platform's authority. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this as an illegitimate invasion of privacy that exceeds the scope of the employment relationship, as these data points are irrelevant to the task of dropping off boxes.

A reasonable person would immediately agree that an automated system acting as a final judge with no human oversight or appeal process is unjust, especially when it factually errs. The 'authority' here (the algorithm) is exercising power it is not competent to hold (unchecked enforcement), making the injustice obvious.

The platform is attempting to exercise employer-level control (exclusivity and device management) over workers it classifies as independent contractors. A reasonable person would immediately recognize this as an illegitimate overreach, as the definition of independent contracting inherently preserves the right to work for multiple clients and control one's own tools.

A reasonable person would immediately agree that a manufacturer cannot retroactively disable physical hardware a user has already purchased outright, especially when the original terms of sale did not disclose a subscription requirement. Asserting ongoing control over sold property constitutes an illegitimate exercise of authority.

A volunteer moderator demanding personal government ID violates standard privacy norms and the platform's own Terms of Service. No reasonable person would accept that a volunteer sub-forum moderator has the legitimate authority to collect sensitive PII that the platform administrators themselves do not require.

A video game's anti-cheat software scanning private tax documents clearly exceeds the jurisdiction granted by the user or the EULA. No reasonable person would argue that a game developer has the authority to surveil unrelated personal financial files.

The authority figure is a hacker who acquired control through theft (phishing) rather than legitimate delegation or community consensus. No reasonable person would recognize a scammer's extortion demands as a valid rule or legitimate governance.

The platform explicitly classifies workers as independent contractors to avoid employer obligations but attempts to exercise employer-level control over means and methods (acceptance rates, signage). A reasonable person immediately recognizes this as an illegitimate exercise of authority because it violates the legal and social definition of the relationship the platform itself established.
